1: And YHWH spoke to Moses — the new revelation formula opens a new legislative unit; ch 17 addresses the blood-slaughter-centralization laws that bridge the Yom Kippur materials and the Holiness Code proper (chs 17–26). The chapter's concern with blood (vv.3-16) and with the theological basis of the prohibition creates one of the most important theological passages in Leviticus.
2: Speak to Aaron and his sons and to all the people of Israel and say to them — the expanded address (Aaron, his sons, all Israel) marks this as a comprehensive law applying to all, not only priests; the blood legislation transcends cultic categories and governs the entire community's relationship to blood.
3: If any one of the house of Israel kills an ox or a lamb or a goat in the camp, or kills it outside the camp — the prohibition covers all slaughter of the three sacrificial species (ox, sheep, goat) both inside and outside the camp. This is the centralization law: all slaughter of these animals must be brought to the sanctuary, not performed in private. The 'outside the camp' specification covers the wilderness context; in the land, the parallel rule will be adapted (Deut 12 permits non-sacrificial slaughter at any location but requires all sacrifice at the central sanctuary).
4: And does not bring it to the entrance of the tent of meeting to offer it as an offering to YHWH in front of the tabernacle of YHWH, bloodguilt shall be imputed to that man (dam yechashev la-ish ha-hu) — unauthorized slaughter of sacrificial animals incurs bloodguilt (dam) as though the person had shed human blood. The severity of the penalty (karet, v.4b) reflects the theological claim that all slaughter of domestic animals belongs to YHWH's sacrificial economy; private killing outside the sanctuary denies YHWH's ownership of animal life.
5: This is to the end that the people of Israel may bring their sacrifices that they sacrifice in the open field (al-peney ha-sadeh), that they may bring them to YHWH, to the priest at the entrance of the tent of meeting — the purpose clause: the law drives all field-slaughter into the sanctuary system. The 'open field' sacrifices are the unauthorized alternative that the centralization law prohibits; the phrase implies that pre-centralization or non-Israelite practice involved field slaughter without priestly mediation.
6: And the priest shall throw the blood on the altar of YHWH at the entrance of the tent of meeting and burn the fat for a pleasing aroma to YHWH — the re-categorized field slaughter becomes a peace offering (shelamim) with standard blood and fat procedures; the formerly unauthorized killing is transformed into legitimate communal worship. This economically validates the centralization law: it is not confiscatory but transformative, integrating all animal slaughter into the covenant fellowship-meal structure.
7: So they shall no more sacrifice their sacrifices to goat demons (la-se'irim asher hem zonim achareihem), after whom they whore. This shall be a statute forever for them throughout their generations — the se'irim (goat-demons, satyrs) are the wilderness spirit-beings to whom unauthorized field sacrifices were directed; the prostitution language (zonim, whoring) frames these offerings as spiritual adultery. The se'irim appear in Isa 13:21 and 34:14 as wilderness spirits; Lev 17:7's reference implies that such field-sacrifices to goat-demons were an actual practice in Israel's wilderness experience (cf. the golden calf incident, Amos 5:25-26).
8: And you shall say to them, Any one of the house of Israel, or of the strangers who sojourn among them, who offers a burnt offering or sacrifice — the centralization law extends to sojourning Gentiles residing in Israel; they are equally prohibited from unauthorized sacrifice. The inclusion of the ger (sojourner) in the same legislation as Israel is consistent throughout Lev 17 (vv.10,12,13,15) and reflects the Holiness Code's principle that the land itself requires protection from all defilement, regardless of the offender's ethnic identity.
9: And does not bring it to the entrance of the tent of meeting to offer it to YHWH, that man shall be cut off from his people — karet for unauthorized sacrifice applies equally to Israelite and sojourner; the sanctity of the centralized sanctuary system is non-negotiable for all who live within the covenant community's territory.
10: If any one of the house of Israel or of the strangers who sojourn among them eats any blood, I will set my face against that person who eats blood and will cut him off from among his people — the blood-eating prohibition is restated with the divine first-person threat (natati panay, I will set my face against) that signals YHWH's direct punitive action rather than communal execution. The prohibition applies universally; the following verse (v.11) provides the theological rationale.
11: For the life (nefesh) of the flesh is in the blood, and I have given it for you on the altar to make atonement for your souls, for it is the blood that makes atonement by the life (ki ha-dam hu ba-nefesh yekhapper) — the foundational theological statement of Levitical sacrifice: blood contains/is the nefesh (life/soul) of the flesh; YHWH has given blood specifically for the altar-atonement function; and it is the blood (qua life-substance) that performs atonement. This three-part statement explains why blood is simultaneously the most sacred substance (YHWH's atonement-medium) and the most prohibited food (it belongs to YHWH). The blood-nefesh identification has no parallel in ANE sacrificial theology; Mesopotamian texts describe gods consuming food offerings but do not articulate a blood-as-life-for-atonement theology.
12: Therefore I have said to the people of Israel, No person among you shall eat blood, neither shall any stranger who sojourns among you eat blood — the deductive conclusion from v.11: because blood is nefesh given for atonement, it may not be eaten. The logical structure (theological premise → behavioral prohibition) is explicit, making Lev 17:11 the most clearly reasoned prohibition in the Levitical law code.
13: Any one also of the people of Israel, or of the strangers who sojourn among them, who takes in hunting any beast or bird that may be eaten shall pour out its blood and cover it with earth (shefakh dam vekissahu be-afar) — the game-animal blood rule: hunted animals (not sacrificeable) still require blood respect; their blood must be poured out and covered with earth (not swallowed, not left exposed). The earth-covering creates a kind of minimal 'burial' of the life-substance; the action acknowledges that even the life of a wild animal belongs to YHWH and cannot simply be consumed or wasted.
14: For the life of every creature is its blood: its blood is its life. Therefore I have said to the people of Israel, You shall not eat the blood of any creature, for the life of every creature is its blood. Whoever eats it shall be cut off — the restatement of v.11's principle extended to all creatures (not only domestic sacrificial animals): nefesh-in-blood is universal. The double formulation (ki nefesh kol-basar damo beenafesho hu) and the triple repetition of the nefesh-blood identification within vv.11-14 emphasize that this is the core theological claim of the chapter.
15: And every person who eats what dies of itself (neveilah) or what is torn by beasts (terefah), whether he is a native or a sojourner, shall wash his clothes and bathe himself in water and be unclean until the evening; then he shall be clean — eating neveilah or terefah creates until-evening impurity (not karet); these animals died without proper blood-drainage through slaughter. The lesser penalty than blood-eating reflects that the prohibition is still operative but the blood has not been deliberately consumed in the same sense as fresh blood eating.
16: But if he does not wash them or bathe his flesh, he shall bear his iniquity — the penalty for failure to perform the required laundering-and-bathing purification after eating neveilah/terefah; bearing iniquity (avono yissa) is a lesser consequence than karet but still indicates personal culpability for ignoring a known purification requirement.
